title: Step Decay
concept: Step decay lowers the learning rate at chosen milestones, keeping updates steady for a while and then dropping them when training needs finer control.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why might a model want a smaller learning rate later in training?

options.text: Because later training often needs finer adjustments near a good solution.
Because smaller rates add more labels to the dataset.
Because learning must stop completely after a few epochs.

exercises.explain: Right. Later training is often more about careful refinement than big leaps.

cards.title: Not a ramp — a staircase
cards.body: Sometimes you do not need a perfectly smooth control knob. A staircase works: stay at one level for a while, then step down when the job changes. That is the idea behind step decay.

cards.title: Drop in stages
cards.body: With step decay, the learning rate stays fixed for a block of training, then drops to a smaller value at a chosen milestone. The network gets a period of bold updates, then a calmer period, then possibly an even calmer one.

cards.title: Shifting gears at checkpoints
cards.body: A cyclist may keep one gear on a flat road, then shift to an easier gear when the climb begins. They do not adjust the gear every second. They change it at clear moments. Step decay does the same thing for training.

cards.title: Epoch 10, then epoch 20
cards.body: Imagine a classifier trains well for 10 epochs with one learning rate, then starts improving more slowly. You can drop the rate at epoch 10, let training continue, and drop it again later if the model now needs more precise updates.

cards.title: Sudden drops are not automatically bad
cards.body: A common myth is that every change in learning rate must be perfectly smooth. But a simple drop can work well. If training is ready for smaller steps, a clean stage change is often enough.

cards.title: Flat stretches with clear drops
cards.body: Step decay is simple because most of the run uses a few clear plateaus instead of constant tiny adjustments.

left.text:
Hold one rate for a while.
Drop at milestones.
Easy to reason about and tune.

right.text:
Never changes at all.
Simpler still, but less adaptable.
May stay too large near the end.

exercises.prompt: What makes step decay different from using one fixed learning rate the whole time?

options.text: It keeps the rate constant for a while, then lowers it at chosen milestones.
It changes the number of classes after each epoch.
It removes the optimizer after the first drop.
It makes every neuron use a different loss function.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Step decay is all about staged drops at clear times.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Step decay changes the learning rate in chunks rather than as one continuous smooth slide.
exercises.explain: True. That chunked, staircase behavior is the defining feature of step decay.

exercises.sentence: Step decay lowers the learning rate in discrete ___.
exercises.explain: Yes. It is a staircase pattern: hold, drop, hold, drop.

exercises.prompt: Which description sounds most like step decay?

options.text: Train at one rate for a while, then lower it at a few planned checkpoints.
Make the rate jump randomly every mini-batch.
Keep the rate identical from the first step to the last.

exercises.explain: Right. Step decay is planned, staged, and easy to visualize as a staircase.

exercises.prompt: You are training a vision model for many epochs. It learns well for a while at one rate, then later needs more delicate updates. Your team wants a schedule that is easy to understand and can be changed at clear checkpoints. Which choice fits best?

options.text: Use step decay so the learning rate drops in stages at chosen milestones.
Use one fixed learning rate forever because later refinement never matters.
Randomize the learning rate every batch with no plan.
Delete the optimizer once the model improves.

exercises.explain: Correct. Step decay is popular precisely because it gives training simple, planned gear shifts.
